one_line_takeaway: A cold-start RL-trained small attacker model (SLINGSHOT) autonomously discovers short 'imperative overloading' prompts that raise Tag-Along jailbreak success against a safety-aligned Operator from 1.7% to 67.0% on held-out extreme tasks, transfers zero-shot to some closed-source models (56.0% ASR on Gemini 2.5 Flash) while failing on others, and is more effective (39.2% ASR) against a prompt-injection-hardened model (Meta-SecAlign-8B) than against its unhardened base (11.5% ASR).
key_finding: On the 41 held-out extreme-difficulty tasks against the source Operator (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-AWQ), SLINGSHOT reaches 67.0% ASR versus a 1.7% Base-A prompting baseline (0.5% for Base-H); Pass@10 rises from 15.0% (Base-A) to 80.4%; pooled Refusal Rate falls from 66.8% to 10.0%; and expected attempts-to-first-success on solved tasks drops from 52.3 to 1.3. Qualitative analysis of trajectories shows SLINGSHOT converges not to multi-turn social engineering but to short (~70-token), instruction-like, imperative-syntax attacks the authors term 'Imperative Overloading' (e.g., mimicking system-prompt/API-instruction phrasing, sometimes appended with a dense positive-affect suffix). A single-turn-with-larger-budget ablation converges to similar peak success (73.1% vs 77.4% for single- vs multi-turn) and similarly short prompts, supporting that the vulnerability is a syntactic 'gap' rather than a persuasion-dependent one. Zero-shot transfer of the single-turn checkpoint (trained only on Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-AWQ) achieves 57.8% ASR on DeepSeek V3.1 and 56.0% ASR on Gemini 2.5 Flash (closed-source), but collapses to near 0% on Llama 4 Maverick, GPT-5 Nano, and Claude 3 Haiku (Llama 4's refusal rate actually rose under attack, 30.1% vs 19.2% baseline), indicating idiosyncratic, non-uniform safety-guardrail coverage across model families. Against Meta-SecAlign-8B, a model explicitly fine-tuned for robustness to prompt injection, SLINGSHOT achieves 39.2% ASR -- markedly higher than the 11.5% ASR against its unmerged base model Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct -- which the paper interprets as evidence that defense specialization against one threat (IPI) can create an 'alignment tax' that increases vulnerability to a different threat (Tag-Along attacks).
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'Tag-Along Attacks': a threat model in tool-augmented multi-agent LLM systems where a tool-less adversary agent manipulates a trusted, tool-privileged 'Operator' agent through conversational interaction alone (no data poisoning, no gradients, no injected content in the environment) to induce execution of normally-refused tool calls. The authors build SLINGSHOT, a black-box, API-only, cold-start reinforcement-learning framework (trained with CISPO -- Clipped Importance Sampling-weight Policy Optimization -- adapted to multi-turn conversations by masking Operator tokens and computing gradients only on the attacker's trajectory) that trains a small open-weight attacker model to autonomously discover attack strategies against a larger safety-aligned Operator inside the AgentDojo environment. The paper also releases TAGALONG-DOJO, a 575-task benchmark (derived from AgentDojo's Workspace, Banking, Travel, and Slack suites) with a reproducible difficulty-filtering protocol that separates capability failures from safety failures and defines a 'Hard' training split (70 tasks) and an 'Extreme' held-out test split (41 tasks).
threat_model: Two-agent black-box setting inside AgentDojo: a large safety-aligned Operator O has exclusive access to privileged tools F and manages a tool-integrated environment (Workspace/Banking/Travel/Slack) containing sensitive data; a smaller adversary S (SLINGSHOT) has no tool access of its own and can influence O only through natural-language conversation turns. The environment's initial state is assumed trusted (no adversarial strings pre-planted), explicitly distinguishing this from indirect prompt injection (IPI), which the paper frames as exploiting instruction/data ambiguity in untrusted content. In Tag-Along Attacks, the Operator correctly recognizes the adversary as an external conversational peer but is functionally obligated to engage with it, and the attacker exploits that functional necessity rather than data confusion. S observes only O's textual responses and truncated tool outputs (no logits, gradients, or environment/Operator internal state). Attack success is defined as a verifiable, deterministic environment check sE(tau) in {0,1}: the Operator executes the ground-truth prohibited tool sequence specified by the malicious task tau.
limitations: The authors state the study is limited to single-modality (text-only) interactions within the AgentDojo environment; they do not test multi-modal agent inputs or other agentic frameworks/environments. The observed 'alignment tax' finding (Meta-SecAlign regression) is based on one defense-tuned model pair and is explicitly flagged by the authors as requiring broader validation to determine whether it is a systematic failure mode of current fine-tuning approaches or an artifact specific to that training recipe. The Extreme test-split difficulty tiers and Refusal Ratio thresholds were calibrated specifically for the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct base model and the paper's judge/Operator setup, which may limit generalization of the benchmark's difficulty stratification to other base models. Several transfer-evaluation cells are marked '-' (missing, due to API constraints) or NaN (Claude 3 Haiku, where zero successes occurred), limiting completeness of the cross-model comparison table. For responsible disclosure, the authors withhold specific successful attack strings against active commercial APIs, meaning independent reproduction of the closed-source transfer results (Gemini, GPT-5 Nano, Claude Haiku) is not fully possible from the released artifacts.

datasets_used: AgentDojo environment (Debenedetti et al., 2024) -- Workspace, Banking, Travel, and Slack suites

TAGALONG-DOJO (introduced in this paper) -- 575 hand-crafted-and-expanded task variants; filtered into a 70-task 'Hard' training split and a 41-task 'Extreme' held-out test split based on a 100-attempt-per-task baseline sweep with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
quantitative_results: SLINGSHOT vs Base-A on 41 extreme held-out tasks (source Operator Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-AWQ): ASR 67.0% vs 1.7%; Pass@10 80.4% vs 15.0%; Refusal Rate 10.0% vs 66.8%; Efficiency 1.3 vs 52.3 attempts-to-first-success
Disabling judge shaping reward (R_shape=0) reduces held-out extreme ASR from 67.0% to 58.5%
Single-turn vs multi-turn ablation peak training success: 73.1% vs 77.4%

Zero-shot transfer ASR of single-turn checkpoint: DeepSeek V3.1 57.8%; Gemini 2.5 Flash 56.0%; Qwen2.5 7B 45.4%; Meta-SecAlign-8B 39.2%; Qwen2.5 14B 19.3%; Qwen2.5 72B AWQ 14.4%; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (unmerged base) 11.5%; Llama 4 Maverick 1.0%; Claude 3 Haiku 0.0%; GPT-5 Nano 0.3%; Meta Llama 70B AWQ 5.6%
Llama 4 Maverick refusal rate rose from 19.2% (Base-A) to 30.1% (Checkpoint) under attack
baselines_compared: Base-H: untuned attacker model with a minimal, non-adversarial system prompt (goal + interaction rules only)
Base-A: untuned attacker model with an adversarial system prompt (role/strategy/stealth instructions)
Meta-SecAlign-8B (Chen et al., 2025) vs its unmerged base model Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, used to test whether IPI-focused defense fine-tuning transfers to Tag-Along robustness
